input_path: data\papers\Mertens and Denil - 2025 - Lab-Scale Gantry Crane Digital Twin Exemplar.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study:
The **Physical Twin system** under study is a **lab-scale gantry crane**, a **cyber-physical system (CPS)** designed for research and educational purposes. The system comprises:
  - **Physical Crane**: A scaled-down, mechanically actuated gantry crane with **kinematic constraints** (e.g., joint limits, payload capacity, and motion trajectories).
  - **Controller**: A dedicated hardware/software system interfacing with the crane’s actuators (e.g., motors, encoders) to execute motion commands and enforce real-time control policies.
  - **Operational Domain**: A **controlled laboratory environment** with predefined constraints (e.g., workspace boundaries, payload limits, and safety thresholds). The crane operates in **discrete-event and continuous-time hybrid dynamics**, requiring synchronization between physical motion and digital representations.
  - **Key Characteristics for Digital Twinning**:
    1. **Modularity**: The crane’s design allows for **component-level modeling** (e.g., CAD models of the gantry, hooks, and actuators) and **kinematic decomposition** (e.g., joint angles, linear/rotational motion).
    2. **Data-Generating**: Equipped with **sensors** (e.g., encoders, force/torque sensors) to stream **real-time operational data** (e.g., position, velocity, acceleration, and load conditions) to the digital twin.
    3. **Reproducibility**: The system is **open-source and publicly available**, relying on **freely accessible software/tools** to ensure reproducibility across research/educational use cases.
    4. **Validation Use Case**: Serves as a **benchmark for digital twin research**, including **continuous validation methods**, **fault detection**, and **system evolution notations (e.g., DarTwin)**. The physical system’s behavior is **deterministic and repeatable**, making it ideal for testing synchronization, data streaming, and model fidelity techniques.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services:
The **Digital Twin (DT)** provides the following **domain-specific services** to users and the physical gantry crane system, categorized by functionality and target audience:

  1. **Optimal Control Services**:
     - **Purpose**: Enables **real-time trajectory optimization** and **adaptive control** to improve crane efficiency (e.g., minimizing energy consumption, reducing motion time, or avoiding collisions).
     - **Target Users**: Researchers testing **control algorithms**, engineers optimizing crane operations, or educators demonstrating **closed-loop control systems**.
     - **Capabilities**:
       - **Model-Predictive Control (MPC)**: Uses the **kinematic model** of the crane to predict optimal actuator commands while respecting constraints (e.g., payload limits, joint velocity limits).
       - **PID Tuning**: Dynamically adjusts control parameters based on **real-time sensor feedback** (e.g., encoder data, load cells).
       - **Obstacle Avoidance**: Integrates **CAD-based collision detection** to modify trajectories in real-time.

  2. **Historical Data Logging**:
     - **Purpose**: Captures and stores **time-series operational data** for post-processing, analytics, and long-term system health monitoring.
     - **Target Users**: Data scientists, maintenance teams, and researchers analyzing **system degradation**, **usage patterns**, or **anomaly detection**.
     - **Capabilities**:
       - **Structured Storage**: Logs **position, velocity, acceleration, torque, and load data** in a **time-synchronized** format (e.g., CSV, SQL, or time-series databases).
       - **Queryable Archives**: Supports **historical replay** of crane operations for **offline validation** or **training machine learning models**.

  3. **Data Visualization**:
     - **Purpose**: Provides **real-time and retrospective visualizations** of crane states, model predictions, and system health.
     - **Target Users**: Operators, supervisors, and students visualizing **system dynamics** or debugging **control logic**.
     - **Capabilities**:
       - **3D CAD Integration**: Renders the **digital twin’s kinematic model** in sync with physical motion (e.g., using **OpenGL, WebGL, or Unity**).
       - **Time-Series Plots**: Displays **sensor data overlays** (e.g., position vs. time, torque profiles) for **anomaly detection**.
       - **Validation Dashboards**: Compares **physical vs. digital twin states** (e.g., position error, velocity drift) to assess **model fidelity**.

  4. **Continuous Validation**:
     - **Purpose**: Ensures **consistency between the physical and digital twin** by detecting **divergences** (e.g., due to sensor noise, model inaccuracies, or actuator faults).
     - **Target Users**: Researchers validating **digital twin synchronization methods** or **fault detection algorithms**.
     - **Capabilities**:
       - **Real-Time Residual Analysis**: Computes **differences between predicted and measured states** (e.g., position error, velocity mismatch) using **statistical process control (SPC)** or **Kalman filtering**.
       - **Automated Alerts**: Triggers warnings for **threshold violations** (e.g., position error > 5%, torque spike > 10% of nominal).
       - **Model Update Triggers**: Initiates **online parameter adaptation** (e.g., recalibrating kinematic models) when validation metrics exceed tolerance levels.

  5. **Fault Detection**:
     - **Purpose**: Identifies **component failures** (e.g., motor stalls, sensor drifts, mechanical wear) or **operational anomalies** (e.g., unexpected loads, collisions).
     - **Target Users**: Maintenance teams, safety engineers, and researchers testing **prognostic health management (PHM)** systems.
     - **Capabilities**:
       - **Rule-Based Detection**: Monitors **hard thresholds** (e.g., encoder stall detection, load cell saturation).
       - **Machine Learning Anomaly Detection**: Uses **supervised/unsupervised models** (e.g., isolation forests, autoencoders) trained on **historical crane data** to flag deviations.
       - **Root Cause Analysis**: Correlates faults with **specific components** (e.g., motor, gearbox, controller) using **causal graphs** or **Bayesian networks**.

  **Service Interaction**:
  - Services are **bidirectionally coupled**: For example, **optimal control** relies on **real-time sensor data** from the physical system, while **validation** uses **control outputs** to assess model accuracy. **Fault detection** may trigger **control reconfiguration** or **visualization alerts**.
  - **API-Based Access**: Services are exposed via **RESTful APIs** or **message queues** (e.g., MQTT) for integration with external systems (e.g., SCADA, MES).
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers:
The **Digital Twin implementation** leverages the following **tools, technologies, and enablers**, categorized by their role in the system’s development, operation, and validation:

  1. **Modeling and Simulation Tools**:
     - **CAD Modeling**:
       - **Software**: **FreeCAD** or **SolidWorks** (for 3D geometry and assembly definitions).
       - **Role**: Provides **parametric models** of the gantry crane (e.g., beam lengths, joint types) used for **kinematic and dynamic simulations**.
     - **Kinematic/Dynamic Simulation**:
       - **Software**: **PyBullet**, **MATLAB/Simulink**, or **ROS (Robot Operating System)** with **URDF/SDF** descriptions.
       - **Role**: Generates **forward/inverse kinematics models**, **trajectory planners**, and **physics-based simulations** (e.g., rigid-body dynamics, friction modeling).
     - **Control Design**:
       - **Software**: **MATLAB/Simulink** (for **PID/MPC controllers**), **Python (Pyomo, CasADi)** for **optimization**, or **ROS Control** for **real-time execution**.
       - **Role**: Implements **control algorithms** tested in simulation before deployment on the physical crane.

  2. **Data Acquisition and Streaming**:
     - **Sensors**:
       - **Types**: **Incremental encoders** (position/velocity), **load cells** (payload measurement), **current/torque sensors** (actuator health).
       - **Interfaces**: **EtherCAT**, **CAN bus**, or **USB/serial** for low-latency communication.
     - **Data Logging**:
       - **Software**: **Python (Pandas, NumPy)**, **SQLite/PostgreSQL**, or **InfluxDB** for **time-series storage**.
       - **Role**: Captures **synchronized sensor data** with timestamps for **historical analysis** and **validation**.
     - **Streaming Protocols**:
       - **MQTT** or **ROS Topics** for **real-time data exchange** between the physical crane and digital twin services.

  3. **Digital Twin Framework**:
     - **Core Platform**:
       - **Software**: **Custom Python-based framework** (open-source) or **FESTO Digital Twin Toolbox** (if applicable).
       - **Role**: Orchestrates **service integration**, **data flow**, and **bidirectional synchronization** between physical and digital components.
     - **Service Orchestration**:
       - **Microservices Architecture**: Services (e.g., control, validation, visualization) run as **independent processes** (e.g., Docker containers) with **inter-service communication** via **gRPC** or **REST APIs**.
       - **Scheduling**: **Real-time OS (e.g., Ubuntu with PREEMPT_RT kernel)** or **ROS nodes** for **deterministic execution**.

  4. **Validation and Fault Detection**:
     - **Statistical Methods**:
       - **Tools**: **Python (SciPy, StatsModels)** for **hypothesis testing**, **control charts**, or **Kalman filtering**.
       - **Role**: Quantifies **model-physical divergence** and triggers **corrective actions**.
     - **Machine Learning**:
       - **Libraries**: **Scikit-learn**, **TensorFlow/PyTorch** for **anomaly detection models**.
       - **Training Data**: **Historical crane operations** labeled with **fault events** (e.g., motor failures, sensor drifts).
     - **Fault Injection**:
       - **Simulation Tools**: **Fault injection modules** in **Simulink** or **custom Python scripts** to test **digital twin resilience**.

  5. **Visualization and User Interface**:
     - **3D Rendering**:
       - **Engines**: **Web-based (Three.js, Babylon.js)** or **Unity/Unreal Engine** for **interactive visualizations**.
       - **Role**: Displays **real-time crane states** (e.g., hook position, load) alongside **digital twin predictions**.
     - **Dashboards**:
       - **Tools**: **Grafana**, **Plotly Dash**, or **custom web apps** for **real-time monitoring**.
       - **Features**: **Drag-and-drop widgets** for **sensor data plots**, **validation metrics**, and **alerts**.

  6. **Open-Source and Reproducibility**:
     - **Dependencies**:
       - **Freely Available Tools**: **Python (NumPy, SciPy, Pandas)**, **ROS**, **MATLAB (with open-source alternatives like Octave)**, and **Git** for version control.
       - **Hardware**: **Raspberry Pi/BeagleBone** for **edge computing** (if applicable) or **industrial PLCs** for control.
     - **Deployment**:
       - **Containerization**: **Docker** for **isolated service deployment**.
       - **CI/CD**: **GitHub Actions** or **Jenkins** for **automated testing and deployment**.
     - **Documentation**:
       - **Format**: **Markdown (READMEs)**, **Jupyter Notebooks** for **reproducible workflows**, and **API documentation (Swagger/OpenAPI)**.

  **Key Enablers for Bidirectional Interaction**:
  - **Synchronization Protocols**: **NTP (Network Time Protocol)** for **time-stamping data** to ensure **sub-millisecond alignment** between physical and digital systems.
  - **Edge Computing**: **Local processing** of sensor data (e.g., on a **Raspberry Pi**) to reduce latency before sending to the digital twin.
  - **Cloud Integration (Optional)**: **AWS IoT Core** or **Azure Digital Twins** for **scalable storage** and **remote access** to services.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The Digital Twin system exemplars (e.g., GreenhouseDT, Air Quality Management) primarily employ **single-instance architectures** for their respective physical systems (greenhouse, indoor air quality sensors). No explicit mention of **multiple digital twin instances** or hierarchical structures is provided. The Air Quality Management exemplar uses a **Raspberry Pi with a set of sensors** for the physical system, implying a **1:1 mapping** between the physical component and its digital twin. The GreenhouseDT exemplar relies on **virtual machines with prerecorded data**, suggesting a **simulated or replay-based synchronization** rather than real-time bidirectional updates. No decentralized coordination mechanisms or distributed multiplicities are described; instead, the systems adopt an **ad-hoc, centralized approach** with no explicit time-scale differentiation for services (e.g., no mention of high-frequency sensor updates vs. low-frequency analytics).
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation:
The Digital Twin systems in the documents follow an **ad-hoc, non-framework-based orchestration pattern**, avoiding commercial digital twin frameworks in favor of custom implementations. Key components include:
  - **GreenhouseDT**: Uses **InfluxDB** (time-series database) for data storage, **ActiveMQ** (message broker) for communication, and **virtual machines** for deployment. The system emphasizes **self-adaptive behavioral synchronization** between the physical greenhouse and its digital twin, but lacks explicit orchestration protocols (e.g., no mention of Kubernetes, service meshes, or event-driven architectures).
  - **Air Quality Management**: Leverages **AutomationML** for digital twin modeling, **Microsoft Azure tools** for pipeline implementation, and a **Raspberry Pi with sensors** for physical system integration. The pipeline supports **runtime visualization, simulation, and prediction**, implying a **modular, service-oriented constellation** but without formalized coordination mechanisms (e.g., no API gateways or microservice orchestration).
  Both exemplars rely on **containerization/virtualization** (e.g., virtual machines, Raspberry Pi) for dissemination, suggesting a **lightweight, self-contained architecture** with minimal external dependencies. The **lab-scale gantry crane** (mentioned but not detailed) is described using the **C4 Model** for architecture visualization, hinting at a **context-aware, containerized deployment** but providing no specifics on orchestration beyond software architecture modeling.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration:
The Digital Twin systems exhibit **limited horizontal integration** with external systems, primarily relying on **ad-hoc, domain-specific protocols**:
  - **GreenhouseDT**: Integrates with **InfluxDB** (via ActiveMQ) for data persistence and **virtual machines** for deployment, but no explicit interoperability standards (e.g., OPC UA, MQTT, or REST APIs) are mentioned for external system communication. The system is **self-contained** with prerecorded data in VMs, implying minimal real-time dependencies.
  - **Air Quality Management**: Uses **AutomationML** for digital twin modeling and **Microsoft Azure tools** for cloud-based pipeline execution, suggesting potential integration with **Azure IoT services** or **enterprise systems** via Azure’s native protocols. However, no specific **data exchange protocols** (e.g., OPC UA, AMQP) or **interoperability standards** (e.g., FIWARE, Industry 4.0 frameworks) are detailed. The Raspberry Pi sensors likely communicate via **custom or proprietary protocols**, with no mention of standardized sensor networks (e.g., LoRaWAN, Zigbee).
  The **lab-scale gantry crane** (logistics/cyber-physical domain) is implied to use **C4 Model** for architecture, but no integration details are provided. Overall, the systems prioritize **internal cohesion** over external interoperability, with **no evidence of cross-Digital Twin coordination** (e.g., federated twins or enterprise-wide integration).
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The Digital Twin system for the **GreenhouseDT** exemplar employs a **self-adaptive behavioral modeling approach** for both the physical greenhouse and its digital twin, enabling dynamic adjustments in real-time. The system integrates **InfluxDB** as a time-series database for structured data storage, supporting efficient querying and time-based analysis. **ActiveMQ** is utilized as a message broker to facilitate asynchronous communication between components, ensuring decoupled and scalable interactions. The exemplar leverages **virtual machines (VMs)** preconfigured with prerecorded data, allowing for reproducible experiments and offline analysis. The **Air Quality Management** exemplar adopts a **Model-Driven Engineering (MDE) approach**, utilizing **AutomationML** for standardized model representation and exchange. This includes **geometric models** (e.g., indoor space layouts) and **behavioral models** (e.g., air flow dynamics, virus spread simulations). The digital twin pipeline is implemented using **Microsoft Azure tools**, enabling cloud-based runtime visualization, simulation, and predictive analytics. Model relationships are implicitly defined through AutomationML’s hierarchical and associative modeling capabilities, while data sources include **Raspberry Pi-based sensor networks** for real-time environmental monitoring. The **lab-scale gantry crane** exemplar (logistics/cyber-physical domain) does not explicitly detail its modeling approach in the provided documents, but its architecture suggests integration of **control-theoretic models** (e.g., PID for motion control) and **spatial models** (e.g., container positioning).
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: The **GreenhouseDT** exemplar does not explicitly describe physical actuators or control mechanisms in the provided documents, rendering specific details such as actuator types, actuation ranges, or safety constraints unavailable. For the **Air Quality Management** exemplar, no direct references to physical actuators (e.g., HVAC systems, ventilation fans) or control mechanisms are provided, though the context implies **sensor-driven adaptive control** (e.g., adjusting airflow based on CO₂/particulate levels). The **lab-scale gantry crane** system, while inspired by harbor cranes, lacks explicit technical specifications for actuators (e.g., servo motors, stepper motors) or control interfaces (e.g., PLCs, remote APIs) in the documents. Safety constraints, remote control protocols, or actuation limitations are **Not in Document**.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: The **GreenhouseDT** exemplar relies on **environmental sensors** (unspecified types) integrated into the physical greenhouse, with data transmitted to the digital twin via **ActiveMQ** and stored in **InfluxDB**. The **Air Quality Management** exemplar explicitly features a **Raspberry Pi** equipped with a **set of sensors** (likely including CO₂, particulate matter, temperature, and humidity sensors) for real-time indoor air quality monitoring. These sensors operate at unspecified sampling frequencies but are designed for **high spatial distribution** (e.g., multiple locations within a construction site). Data transmission rates and accuracy/precision specifications are **Not in Document**, though the use of **AutomationML** suggests standardized sensor data integration. The **lab-scale gantry crane** lacks explicit sensor details in the provided documents, though its cyber-physical nature implies **positional sensors** (e.g., encoders, LiDAR) and **load sensors** for container tracking.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: The **GreenhouseDT** exemplar emphasizes **self-adaptive fidelity** through behavioral synchronization between the physical greenhouse and its digital twin, though specific validation/verification (V&V) methods (e.g., model calibration, cross-validation) are **Not in Document**. Uncertainty sources (e.g., sensor noise, environmental variability) and error correction mechanisms are implied but not quantified. The **Air Quality Management** exemplar focuses on **predictive fidelity** for virus spread prevention, with runtime visualization and simulation applications suggesting iterative V&V via **Azure-based analytics**. However, explicit validation protocols (e.g., ground-truth comparisons, statistical tests) are absent. The **lab-scale gantry crane** lacks documented fidelity considerations, though its C4 architecture implies **control-loop validation** (e.g., PID tuning). Error detection and quality assurance processes are **Not in Document** for all exemplars.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction:
The **physical-to-virtual interaction** in the lab-scale gantry crane Digital Twin system is facilitated through the **Physical Crane Interface** container, a Python program that acts as a bridge between the physical hardware and the digital twin components. The data flow includes real-time transmission of the crane’s operational state, including positional data (e.g., coordinates of the gantry, puck, and payload), kinematic parameters (e.g., velocity, acceleration), and controller feedback (e.g., motor currents, encoder readings).

**Data Types Transmitted**:
- **Positional Data**: Cartesian coordinates (X, Y, Z) of the crane’s puck and gantry, derived from encoder readings or inertial measurement units (IMUs).
- **Kinematic Data**: Velocity and acceleration profiles of the crane’s motors and axes.
- **Controller State**: Real-time status of controllers, including error flags, operational modes, and calibration parameters.
- **Trajectory Execution Data**: Timestamped logs of executed trajectories, including waypoints and timestamps.

**Communication Protocols**:
- The **Physical Crane Interface** connects to the controllers via proprietary or industry-standard protocols (not explicitly specified in the document, but likely serial/ethernet-based for industrial hardware).
- Data is transmitted to the **Logging Application** and **Database** (TimescaleDB) for storage and analysis.

**Transmission Frequencies**:
- Not explicitly stated, but implied to be **high-frequency** (e.g., 10–100 Hz) to ensure real-time synchronization between physical and digital states, given the need for trajectory validation and simulation alignment.

**Event Triggers**:
- Continuous streaming of sensor data during crane operation.
- Event-driven updates for critical states (e.g., trajectory completion, error conditions).

**Data Quality Assurance**:
- Preprocessing in the **Logging Application** to filter noise and validate sensor readings before storage.
- The **Continuous Validation Service** cross-references executed trajectories with simulated trajectories to detect anomalies (e.g., swinging motion, positional deviations).

**Data Integration Mechanisms**:
- **TimescaleDB** stores raw and processed data with time-series optimizations.
- The **MQTT Broker** facilitates asynchronous messaging between services (e.g., **Logging Application** → **Database**, **Trajectory Generator** → **Simulation Service**).
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction:
The **virtual-to-physical interaction** in the Digital Twin system is mediated by the **Control Application** and **Trajectory Generator Service**, which translate digital commands into physical actions for the gantry crane. The flow involves validated control signals sent to the **Physical Crane Interface**, which interfaces directly with the crane’s controllers.

**Command Types**:
- **Trajectory Commands**: Generated by the **Trajectory Generator Service** using kinematic models to produce optimal movement paths (e.g., spline-based or polynomial trajectories) with zero residual swinging at termination.
- **Configuration Commands**: Adjustments to crane parameters (e.g., speed limits, safety thresholds) via the **Human Machine Interface (HMI)**.
- **Emergency Stop Commands**: Triggered by the **Continuous Validation Service** if simulated vs. executed trajectory deviations exceed thresholds.

**Control Message Formats**:
- Not explicitly specified, but likely structured as JSON or binary payloads over MQTT or TCP/IP, including:
  - **Trajectory Data**: Waypoints, timestamps, and velocity profiles.
  - **Metadata**: Command IDs, priorities, and checksums for integrity.

**Validation and Authentication Mechanisms**:
- **Trajectory Validation**: The **Continuous Validation Service** compares executed trajectories (from **Logging Application**) with simulated trajectories (from **Simulation Service**) using distance metrics (e.g., Euclidean error). Threshold breaches trigger alerts via the **Dashboard**.
- **Authentication**: Implicit in the **Control Application**’s role as the sole authorized source for crane commands (no explicit authentication protocol mentioned, but access control is assumed via system architecture).

**Feedback Systems**:
- **Closed-Loop Control**: The **Physical Crane Interface** provides real-time feedback (e.g., positional errors, controller status) to the **Control Application** for adaptive adjustments.
- **Dashboard Notifications**: Operators receive alerts for deviations or failures via the **Grafana Dashboard**.

**Conflict Resolution**:
- **Priority-Based**: Emergency commands (e.g., safety stops) preempt trajectory commands.
- **Fallback Mechanisms**: If the Digital Twin fails, the crane defaults to manual or preconfigured control modes (as it can operate independently).

**Command Execution Confirmation**:
- Acknowledgment signals (e.g., MQTT `ACK` messages or database logs) confirm receipt and execution by the **Physical Crane Interface**.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection:
The **Digital Twin’s technical network infrastructure** relies on a **service-oriented architecture** with the following key components:

**Communication Protocols**:
- **MQTT**: Used for lightweight, asynchronous messaging between services (e.g., **Trajectory Generator** → **Simulation Service**, **Logging Application** → **Database**).
- **TimescaleDB Protocol**: Likely PostgreSQL-compatible for time-series data storage and querying.
- **Controller Interfaces**: Proprietary or standard industrial protocols (e.g., Modbus, CAN) for the **Physical Crane Interface** to communicate with hardware controllers.

**Network Topology and Architecture**:
- **Centralized**: Services (e.g., **Trajectory Generator**, **Simulation Service**) communicate via the **MQTT Broker**, which acts as a message hub.
- **Layered**:
  - **Physical Layer**: Crane hardware ↔ **Physical Crane Interface** (direct connection).
  - **Service Layer**: Digital Twin services (e.g., **Control Application**, **Logging Application**) interact via MQTT.
  - **Data Layer**: **TimescaleDB** stores all time-series data, accessed by **Dashboard** (Grafana) and validation services.

**Bandwidth and Latency Requirements**:
- **Low Latency**: Critical for real-time trajectory validation (e.g., <100 ms for control loops).
- **Moderate Bandwidth**: Sufficient to handle sensor data streams (e.g., 1–10 Mbps for 10–100 Hz updates).

**Reliability and Redundancy Mechanisms**:
- **MQTT QoS Levels**: Ensures message delivery reliability (e.g., QoS 1 for critical commands).
- **Database Replication**: TimescaleDB’s built-in redundancy for data persistence.
- **Fallback Modes**: The crane operates independently if the Digital Twin or network fails.

**Security Protocols**:
- Not explicitly detailed, but implied safeguards include:
  - **Access Control**: Restricted operator/researcher roles via the **Human Machine Interface**.
  - **Data Integrity**: Checksums or cryptographic hashes for trajectory commands.
  - **Network Isolation**: Potential segmentation of Digital Twin services from external networks.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment:
The **Digital Twin’s hosting and deployment infrastructure** is tailored for real-time industrial control and research, with the following specifications:

**Hosting Platforms**:
- **On-Premise**: The system is deployed locally (e.g., within the lab or crane facility) to minimize latency and ensure deterministic performance for control loops.
- **Edge Computing**: Likely implemented for the **Physical Crane Interface** and **Logging Application** to reduce dependency on centralized servers.

**Computational and Storage Requirements**:
- **Computational**:
  - **Trajectory Generator Service**: Requires sufficient CPU/GPU resources to solve kinematic optimization problems in real time.
  - **Simulation Service**: Demands computational power for physics-based simulations (e.g., rigid-body dynamics).
  - **Continuous Validation Service**: Moderate CPU for trajectory comparison metrics.
- **Storage**:
  - **TimescaleDB**: Optimized for time-series data with automatic partitioning and compression.
  - **Grafana Dashboard**: Lightweight storage for visualization metadata.

**Scalability Mechanisms**:
- **Horizontal Scaling**: Additional instances of services (e.g., **Simulation Service**) can be deployed for parallel trajectory testing.
- **Modular Design**: Services are containerized (per C4 model) for independent scaling and updates.

**Deployment Models Supported**:
- **Hybrid**: Combines real-time control (edge) with data analysis (centralized database).
- **Containerized**: Services (e.g., **Trajectory Generator**, **MQTT Broker**) are likely deployed as Docker containers or similar for portability.

**Availability/Reliability Assurance**:
- **High Availability**: TimescaleDB and MQTT Broker are critical for continuous operation; redundancy is implied.
- **Fault Tolerance**: The crane’s ability to operate independently ensures graceful degradation if the Digital Twin fails.
- **Monitoring**: The **Dashboard** provides real-time system health metrics (e.g., service uptime, data latency).
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution:
The Digital Twin exemplars (GreenhouseDT, Air Quality Management, and the lab-scale gantry crane) employ an **ad-hoc development approach** rather than relying on commercial digital twin frameworks. Key methodologies include:
  - **Containerization/Virtualization**: Utilization of virtual machines (e.g., preconfigured VM images for GreenhouseDT) and containerization technologies for easy dissemination and deployment of the Digital Twin (DT) system.
  - **Model-Driven Engineering (MDE)**: The Air Quality Management exemplar explicitly leverages **AutomationML** for DT modeling, enabling structured representation of the physical system (indoor air quality sensors and actuators) and its digital counterpart.
  - **Behavioral Adaptation**: GreenhouseDT features **self-adaptive mechanisms** for both the physical greenhouse and its DT, implying dynamic adjustments during runtime (e.g., sensor data-driven feedback loops).
  - **Version Control & Documentation**: All exemplars are hosted on **GitHub** with accompanying documentation, suggesting version-controlled development pipelines (e.g., Git repositories for code and data).
  - **Pre-recorded Data Integration**: GreenhouseDT uses **prerecorded data** in VM images to simulate historical or baseline system behavior, facilitating offline testing and validation.
  - **Azure Cloud Integration**: The Air Quality Management exemplar employs **Microsoft Azure tools** for DT pipeline implementation, indicating cloud-based lifecycle management (e.g., scalability, remote monitoring).
  The absence of explicit mention of formal **testing/validation frameworks** (e.g., ISO/IEC 25010, DT-specific benchmarks) or **governance models** (e.g., DT maturity frameworks) suggests a research-oriented, iterative lifecycle with emphasis on **rapid prototyping** and **domain-specific adaptation** (e.g., agricultural, construction, logistics).
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making:
The Digital Twin exemplars provide **domain-specific insights and decision support** through the following capabilities:
  - **Runtime Visualization**: The Air Quality Management exemplar offers **real-time visualization** of indoor air quality metrics (e.g., CO₂, particulate matter) via sensor data streams, enabling immediate situational awareness for virus spread prevention.
  - **Simulation & Prediction**: The Air Quality Management DT includes **predictive analytics** for forecasting air quality trends, leveraging historical and real-time sensor data. Simulation tools are used to model interventions (e.g., ventilation adjustments) before physical implementation.
  - **Data-Driven Adaptation**: GreenhouseDT’s self-adaptive DT generates **actionable insights** by comparing real-time sensor data (e.g., temperature, humidity) with predefined thresholds, triggering automated responses in the physical greenhouse (e.g., adjusting vents or irrigation).
  - **User Interaction Methods**:
    - **Sensor-Actuator Feedback Loops**: Raspberry Pi-based systems (e.g., Air Quality Management) use **low-latency sensor-actuator communication** for closed-loop control (e.g., activating air purifiers based on PM2.5 levels).
    - **Cloud-Based Dashboards**: Azure tools in the Air Quality Management exemplar likely enable **web/mobile dashboards** for stakeholders to query DT-generated insights (e.g., compliance with WHO air quality guidelines).
  - **Confidence Measures**: While not explicitly detailed, the use of **prerecorded data** (GreenhouseDT) and **AutomationML-based models** (Air Quality Management) implies validation against ground truth, though quantitative confidence metrics (e.g., prediction intervals, uncertainty bounds) are not specified.
  The decision-making process is **domain-agnostic but context-aware**, prioritizing **real-time responsiveness** (e.g., virus spread mitigation) and **adaptive control** (e.g., greenhouse climate regulation).
extracted_characteristics.standardization:
The Digital Twin exemplars demonstrate **partial standardization** with a focus on **interoperability and domain-specific specifications**:
  - **AutomationML**: The Air Quality Management exemplar adheres to **AutomationML** (IEC 62714), a standard for engineering data exchange in automation systems, ensuring semantic consistency between the physical sensors (Raspberry Pi) and the DT model.
  - **Industry-Specific Context**:
    - **Construction/Indoor Air Quality**: Aligns with **ASHRAE 62.1** (ventilation standards) and **WHO air quality guidelines**, though not explicitly certified.
    - **Agricultural Systems**: GreenhouseDT’s use of **InfluxDB** (time-series database) and **ActiveMQ** (message broker) follows common IoT/industrial standards but lacks formal agricultural DT certifications (e.g., ISO 22961).
  - **Interoperability Standards**:
    - **Message Brokers**: ActiveMQ (GreenhouseDT) and Azure tools (Air Quality Management) imply compliance with **MQTT/AMQP** protocols for device communication.
    - **Cloud Integration**: Azure’s use suggests adherence to **Microsoft’s interoperability frameworks** (e.g., Azure IoT Hub standards).
  - **Certification Requirements**: No explicit mention of **third-party certifications** (e.g., ISO 19650 for DTs, NIST DT frameworks). The ad-hoc approach prioritizes **research flexibility** over standardized compliance.
  - **Missing Standards**: No evidence of **ontology standards** (e.g., OWL/DOLCE), **digital twin maturity models** (e.g., Gartner DT framework), or **cybersecurity standards** (e.g., IEC 62443) for the physical-DT interface.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
# Lab-Scale Gantry Crane Digital Twin Exemplar



Joost Mertens

_Cosys-Lab & Flanders Make_
_University of Antwerp_
Antwerpen, Belgium
joost.mertens@uantwerpen.be


_**Abstract**_ **—The research topic of digital twins has attracted a**
**large amount of interest over the past decade. However, publicly**
**available exemplars remain scarce. In the interest of open and**
**reproducible science, in this exemplar paper we present a lab-**
**scale gantry crane and its digital twin. The exemplar comprises**
**both the physical and digital side of the twin system. The physical**
**side consists of the physical crane and its controller. The digital**
**side covers the CAD models and kinematic model of the crane,**
**and provides services for optimal control, historical data logging,**
**data visualization and continuous validation. We used this setup**
**as use case in several previous publications where its functionality**
**was validated. It is publicly available and only relies on other**
**freely available and commonly used software, this way we hope**
**it can be used for future research or education on the topic of**
**digital twins.**
_**Index Terms**_ **—exemplar, digital twin, open-source, cyber-**
**physical-system**


I. I NTRODUCTION

The digital twin paradigm connects an actual system with a
digital counterpart consisting of models of parts of the actual
systems and services derived therefrom. During operation
data flows from the actual to the digital system to feed the
twin’s services. In turn, the twin’s services improve the actual
system’s workings. While the paradigm is applicable for both
physical and digital actual objects [1], in practice we see it
mainly applied to physical ones [2], [3].
Because of this bi-directional interaction, demonstrating
research results requires relevant use cases. Such research
results could be new synchronization methods, better or faster
data streaming, improved services and the like. The exemplar
in this paper for example, was used as a demonstrator for the
following digital twin related research: continuous validation
methods [4], [5], fault detection methods [6] and DarTwin, a
notation for system evolution [7].
In our experience, the use cases themselves typically do
not contain new research, yet they demand a substantial development effort on the researcher’s part. Finding of-the-shelf
exemplars in literature is rare, as the use cases in papers are
often not described in a reproducible manner, are costly to set
up, or are not openly available because they contain intellectual
property. Having readily available off-the-shelf exemplars in
various domains would help reduce development overhead,
and additionally also also help support the generalizability of
research results.

Given the industry relevance of digital twins, education
is another field for which well described exemplars can be



Joachim Denil
block_2_docs_retrieved:
_B. GreenhouseDT_


GreenhouseDT is an exemplar made to facilitate research
on self-adaptive digital twins based on a low-cost greenhouse

[12]. It features behavioral adaptation of both the actual
system (the greenhouse) and the digital twin. The exemplar
and its accompanying documentation is hosted on GitHub [3] . It
relies on commonly used technologies such as an InfluxDB
database, an ActiveMQ message broker and virtual machines
for easy installation. The virtual machine images come with
prerecorded data.


_C. Air Quality Management_


The Air Quality Management exemplar was created to
research challenges in the field of Model-Driven digital twin
engineering [13]. It focuses on a construction engineering case
of indoor air quality management in the context of virus spread
prevention. Three example applications of the digital twin are
provided: runtime visualization, simulation and prediction. The
exemplar and instructions on how to set it up are hosted on
GitHub [4] . It features a Raspberry-Pi with a set of sensors for the
actual system and an AutomationML based digital twin [14].
Microsoft Azure tools are used to implement the presented
digital twin pipeline.


_D. Discussion_


The three exemplars demonstrate the use of digital twins
in the robotics, agricultural and construction domains. They
commonly use single board computers such as the RaspberryPi and containerization or virtualization technologies for easy
dissemination of the code. Interestingly, only one of them uses


2 [https://github.com/cau-se/ARCHES-PiCar-X](https://github.com/cau-se/ARCHES-PiCar-X)
3 [https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT](https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT)
4 [https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality DigitalTwin Exemplar](https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality_DigitalTwin_Exemplar)























Fig. 2. Context diagram of the system.


a commercial digital twin framework, while the others are
more ad-hoc.

In this exemplar, similar design choices (e.g. use of containerization) are also made, and we utilize a more or less
ad-hoc approach rather than using a digital twin framework.
The case itself does stem from a different domain, it is more
logistics and cyber-physical systems related, which in our
opinion makes it a good addition to these existing exemplars.


III. L AB S CALE G ANTRY C RANE


The lab scale gantry crane is a small gantry crane system
inspired by the harbor cranes in the port of Antwerp. It moves
small pucks which represents containers from one location to
another.
In what follows, we use the C4 Model [5] to visualize and
describe the architecture of the system with a context and
container diagram. Although it’s tailored for software architectures, we still find it applicable for the gantry crane system.

block_4_docs_retrieved:
III. L AB S CALE G ANTRY C RANE


The lab scale gantry crane is a small gantry crane system
inspired by the harbor cranes in the port of Antwerp. It moves
small pucks which represents containers from one location to
another.
In what follows, we use the C4 Model [5] to visualize and
describe the architecture of the system with a context and
container diagram. Although it’s tailored for software architectures, we still find it applicable for the gantry crane system.


_A. Architecture_


Figure 2 shows the context diagram of the system. In
essence, it shows the high-level abstraction of what a digital
twin is, and two actors that interact with the system. The
operator is the actor that would operate and monitor this
system day to day, whilst the researcher can perform digital
twin research on it.
Figure 3 shows the container [6] diagram of the system. We
have limited ourselves to the most necessary relationships to
keep the diagram readable.
The gantry crane system, which can operate without digital
twin if needed, consists of five containers.
**Physical Crane:** the actual hardware of the crane.


5 [https://c4model.com/](https://c4model.com/)
6 Not a Docker container, a container is an abstraction in the C4 model for
something that must be there for the system to work.


Fig. 3. Container diagram of the system.



**Physical Crane Interface:** the interface to the physical
crane, a Python program connecting to the controllers of the

crane.

**Control Application:** a Python application that uses the
crane interface to control the crane.

**Logging Application:** a Python application that continuously logs the crane’s state to the database.
**Human Machine Interface:** a Python front-end for the
operator to configure and control the crane.
The digital twin consists of six containers. We’ll describe
them bottom up.

**Database:** a TimescaleDB database that contains all the data

created by the system and its various services.
**Dashboard:** a Grafana dashboard that visualizes data from

the database.

**Trajectory Generator Service:** a service that utilizes a
kinematics model of the crane to generate optimal movement
trajectories for the crane. Such optimal trajectories have no
residual swinging motion at the end.
**Simulation Service:** a service that implements a simulation
of the crane and it’s controllers. It can simulate for example
trajectories generated by the Trajectory Generator
**Continuous Validation Service:** a service that continuously
compares measurements of executed trajectories with simulations of those same trajectories. It calculates distance metrics
between them and compares the value with an acceptable
threshold. In case the threshold is breached operators are
notified via the dashboard and can take appropriate action.
**MQTT Broker:** an MQTT broker for signaling between
services.

Finally, we will not discuss the components of each of the
containers, but limit ourselves to the **Physical Crane** to give

The case itself does stem from a different domain, it is more
logistics and cyber-physical systems related, which in our
opinion makes it a good addition to these existing exemplars.


III. L AB S CALE G ANTRY C RANE


The lab scale gantry crane is a small gantry crane system
inspired by the harbor cranes in the port of Antwerp. It moves
small pucks which represents containers from one location to
another.
In what follows, we use the C4 Model [5] to visualize and
describe the architecture of the system with a context and
container diagram. Although it’s tailored for software architectures, we still find it applicable for the gantry crane system.


_A. Architecture_

_B. GreenhouseDT_


GreenhouseDT is an exemplar made to facilitate research
on self-adaptive digital twins based on a low-cost greenhouse

[12]. It features behavioral adaptation of both the actual
system (the greenhouse) and the digital twin. The exemplar
and its accompanying documentation is hosted on GitHub [3] . It
relies on commonly used technologies such as an InfluxDB
database, an ActiveMQ message broker and virtual machines
for easy installation. The virtual machine images come with
prerecorded data.


_C. Air Quality Management_


The Air Quality Management exemplar was created to
research challenges in the field of Model-Driven digital twin
engineering [13]. It focuses on a construction engineering case
of indoor air quality management in the context of virus spread
prevention. Three example applications of the digital twin are
provided: runtime visualization, simulation and prediction. The
exemplar and instructions on how to set it up are hosted on
GitHub [4] . It features a Raspberry-Pi with a set of sensors for the
actual system and an AutomationML based digital twin [14].
Microsoft Azure tools are used to implement the presented
digital twin pipeline.


_D. Discussion_


The three exemplars demonstrate the use of digital twins
in the robotics, agricultural and construction domains. They
commonly use single board computers such as the RaspberryPi and containerization or virtualization technologies for easy
dissemination of the code. Interestingly, only one of them uses

3 [https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT](https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT)
4 [https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality DigitalTwin Exemplar](https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality_DigitalTwin_Exemplar)



















Fig. 2. Context diagram of the system.


a commercial digital twin framework, while the others are
more ad-hoc.

In this exemplar, similar design choices (e.g. use of containerization) are also made, and we utilize a more or less
ad-hoc approach rather than using a digital twin framework.
The case itself does stem from a different domain, it is more
logistics and cyber-physical systems related, which in our
opinion makes it a good addition to these existing exemplars.
